ground_truth.title: The best live TV streaming services to cut cable in 2025
ground_truth.author: Amy Skorheim
ground_truth.main_content: Sports fans who want to catch every last game and news junkies who want to watch network news as it unfolds have two main options: cable or a live TV streaming service. Cable or satellite service often requires extra equipment and convoluted contracts. Live TV streaming services only need an internet connection and a smart TV. These plans also used to be cheaper than cable, but rate hikes have put many of them at $85 and above, so the price gap has narrowed. We tested out all the major players to compare what you get and help you make sense of your options. Here are the best live TV streaming services, according to our testing. YouTube TV: Price $83/mo. and up, Simultaneous at-home streams: 3, Total channels: 100+ (base plan), DVR limits: Unlimited, 9 mo. expiration, Multiview: Yes (select programming), Contract: No, Free trial: Yes (length varies). In mid-December of 2024, subscribers got a bummer of an email letting them know the price of YouTube TV would be going up yet again, this time to $83, which is a $10 hike from the last increase of $8 in March of 2023. Even still, Google's option makes a strong case for delivering the best streaming service for live TV. Compared to our top pick for sports, YouTube TV covers major and minor teams, regional games and national matchups almost as well. It gives you clear navigation, a great search function, unlimited DVR and broad network coverage. But, even beyond the price increase, YTTV is financially precarious if you're not great at resisting temptation. Upon signup, you're presented with nearly 50 different add-ons, including 4K resolution, premium channels and themed packages. Even if you fight the urge to roll Max, Shudder and AcornTV into the mix at signup, the enticement remains as it's dangerously easy to add more to your subscription. Fubo: Price $56/mo. and up, Simultaneous at-home streams: 10, Total channels: 215 (base plan), DVR limits: Unlimited, 9 mo. expiration, Multiview: Yes (select programming), Contract: No, Free trial: Yes (length varies). For that reason and others, our top pick for live sports coverage is Fubo. When you first sign up, it asks which teams you follow across all kinds of associations. Pick teams from in-season leagues and you'll quickly have DVR content to watch. That's because Fubo records every game your chosen teams play as long as it's aired on a supported channel - and its sports coverage is vast. I tested out the top-tier package and the guide said there were 118 sports networks to choose from. In addition to the usual suspects from ESPN, Fox, NBC and CBS, you can watch motorsports, international leagues, adventure sports and even poker. Add-ons give you NBA TV, NHL Network, NFL Red Zone and MLB Network. Hulu + Live TV: Price $90/mo. and up, Simultaneous at-home streams: 2, Total channels: 95+ (base plan), DVR limits: Unlimited, 9 mo. expiration, Multiview: No, Contract: No, Free trial: Yes (3 days). Those price jumps are a little easier to take when you consider that you get a lot of content you can't find elsewhere; the service comes bundled with Disney+, ESPN+ and includes Hulu's original programming. So if you're already a Hulu and Disney+ subscriber, it probably makes the most sense for you to go with Hulu's live component. Note that $90 gets you that content with ads - for ad-free Disney+ and Hulu, it's $100 monthly. DirecTV: Price $0 - $170, Simultaneous at-home streams: Varies by plan, Total channels: 75+ (Live TV package), DVR limits: Varies, Multiview: Yes, Contract: No (but has multi-year commitment options), Free trial: Yes. DirecTV Stream offers the most channel variety of all the services we tested, and if you want access to regional sports networks or specific cable channels that other providers don't carry, this might be your best bet. Sling TV: Price $46 and up, Simultaneous at-home streams: 1-3 (depending on plan), Total channels: 32-50+ (base plans), DVR limits: 50 hours (upgradeable), Multiview: No, Contract: No, Free trial: Yes (3 days). Sling TV is the most affordable live TV streaming service that still offers a decent selection of channels. The trade-off is that you get fewer channels overall, and you may miss some regional sports and local broadcast channels depending on your location.
with: Sports fans who want to catch every last game and news junkies who want to watch network news as it unfolds have two main options: cable or a live TV streaming service.
YouTube TV covers major and minor teams, regional games and national matchups almost as well.
Our top pick for live sports coverage is Fubo because it records every game your chosen teams play as long as it's aired on a supported channel.
Hulu + Live TV comes bundled with Disney+, ESPN+ and includes Hulu's original programming.
Sling TV is the most affordable live TV streaming service that still offers a decent selection of channels.
